g.data-narration: Demand paging loads pages into memory only when processes explicitly request them, avoiding upfront loading.
g.text: A lazy loading memory scheme where pages are brought into RAM only when
a process attempts to access them. Executable stays on disk until needed.
✓ Only active pages use RAM → More processes can run simultaneously

g.data-narration: The virtual address space shows pages on disk, while physical RAM contains only currently used frames.
g.text: 2. Virtual Address Space vs Physical Memory

g.data-narration: Each page table entry has a valid-invalid bit. V means page is in RAM; I means page is on disk or invalid.
g.text: When CPU accesses an 'I' page → PAGE FAULT trap to OS

g.data-narration: Page fault handling involves ten coordinated steps from memory access to instruction restart.
Steps 1-5 cover memory access, detecting the invalid page, verifying legality, and finding a free frame.
Steps 6-10 involve blocking the process, waiting for I/O, updating the page table, and restarting.
EAT formula weighs memory access time against page fault probability and disk service time.
g.text: Performance depends on page fault rate (p):
EAT = (1 - p) × ma + p × (Page Fault Overhead)

g.data-narration: Example shows why page faults are catastrophic: just 1 in a million faults causes 100x slowdown.
g.text: → High page fault rate causes THRASHING (system spends more time paging than executing)

g.data-narration: Copy-on-Write lets parent and child share pages initially, copying only when a process writes to a shared page.
g.text: Problem: fork() duplicates all pages immediately → wasteful if exec() follows.
✓ Saves memory & time, especially before exec()

g.data-narration: When no free frames exist, page replacement evicts a victim page, writing it back to disk if modified.
g.text: When all frames occupied and page fault occurs:
Algorithms: FIFO, Optimal, LRU (Least Recently Used) - goal: minimize future page faults

g.data-narration: Demand paging enables larger programs, better CPU utilization, and faster startup, but risks thrashing.
g.text: • Efficient memory use - more processes in RAM
• Run programs larger than physical memory
• Faster startup - load only first pages
• Thrashing if page fault rate too high

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: lazy loading pages on demand, page fault handling sequence, EAT sensitivity to faults, and COW optimization.
This diagram covered demand paging fundamentals, page fault handling, performance calculations, and memory optimization techniques.
g.text: Core technique for modern OS memory management • Pages loaded only when accessed
Page Fault Handling → EAT Calculations → Copy-on-Write → Page Replacement
📝 Remember: Keep page fault rate below 0.0001% for good performance!
